metadata.title: Theming for terror: Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda

abstract: Research has shown that adornment strategies such as "theming" are a prominent and patterned phenomenon in institutional organizational life. Scholars have yet to investigate if, and how, extra-institutional organizations such as terrorist organizations similarly utilize theming as a strategy to signal to their primary consumer base: recruits. To remedy this, we utilize a variety of computational topic modeling techniques built from Latent Dirichlet Analysis (LDA) to analyze a corpus of propaganda aimed at Western audiences. Drawing on the markets-as-politics paradigm we show how Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP) and the Islamic State organization (ISIS) maintain similar strategies of signaling global illegitimacy while cultivating distinct themes of terrorism in an effort to differentiate themselves from one another. We argue that terrorist organizations use propaganda to signal distinct organizational identity, even while broadly operating under the umbrella identity of Islamic fundamentalism. This focus on extra-legal organizations elaborates upon fundamental assumption of markets-as-politics and problematizes prior research asserting that strategies utilized by terrorist organizations to convey meaning are fundamentally the same.

sections.paper_id: Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
paragraphs: There is a marked tendency among contemporary organizations to communicate organizational identity through the strategic use of signals. Images, sounds, and text all demonstrate to audiences that an organization adheres to a certain "theme" (Simonson & Schmitt, 2009). In contrast to other forms of broader organizational identity expression such as branding, "themes provide a concentrated unified experience" to signal identity through the strategic use of design and text (Sallaz, 2012:100). Traditionally, research analyzing theming practices theorized that organizations either strategically construct themes to attract customers (Pine & Gilmore et al., 1999;Schechter, 1993) or adopt themes in order to comply with converging norms of practice (Meyer & Rowan, 1977;DiMaggio & Powell, 1983). More recently, scholars have suggested that organizations leverage multiple themes to distinguish themselves from primary market competitors while also signaling legitimacy to the arbiters of specific political and cultural fields (Fligstein, 1996, Sallaz, 2012, Fligstein & Calder, 2001). This markets-as-politics perspective argues that formal organizations package themes or "adorn" themselves in order to signal identity to key audiences (e.g. the state, potential consumers, other market participants etc.), and "establish the rules and standards by which market participants are judged" in an institutional field (Sallaz, 2012:100).

Yet, little is known about theming in the extra-institutional field made up of illicit or illegitimate organizations such as cartels and terrorist cells. This particular environment is characterized by unclear or contested notions of organizational legitimacy (Bakker, Raab, & Brinton Milward, 2012;Schoon, 2014;Schoon, 2016), and typically inhabited by category spanning organizations (Olzak, 2016). As a result, it behooves scholars to investigate how temporal and spatial complexity pressures illegitimate actors to seek distinction within the "new terrorist society" (Knorr Cetina, 2005). Thus, we ask, how do prominent terrorist organizations adorn themselves in an effort to appeal to the same set of potential recruits? To answer this, we investigate if and how Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP) and the Islamic State organization (ISIS) mirror the theming processes found in the institutional environment as a strategy to cope with this particular type of complex uncertainty.

In this paper, we argue that deviant organizations target specific populations to signal alternative forms of legitimacy than those condoned by states. In essence, terrorist groups acquire legitimation in ways that run counter to norms of behavior, moral boundaries, and acceptable practice. Global terrorist organizations make use of a variety of online propaganda in order to radicalize and motivate potential recruits in Western nations (Aly, Macdonald, Jarvis, & Chen, 2017;Conway, 2017;Rudner, 2017). Online Jihadist magazines and social media serve as a medium of "electronic jihad" waged against perceived enemies (Mair, 2017). These magazines provide signals to consumers as to what type of jihadist organization the group aims to be with sections such as "Make a Bomb in the Kitchen of Your Mom" and "The Jihadi Experiences: Preparation". These organizations offer a proximate ideal-type to understand how illegitimate organizations use theming to negotiate a social space of recruitment.

This argument is furthered by leveraging the power of natural language processing techniques aimed at quantitatively distilling the structure and patterns of text to explore the contours of these magazines using a topic modeling approaches built from Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei, Ng, Jordan, & Lafferty, 2003). LDA is a relational and probabilistic topic modeling procedure that identifies the co-occurrence of prominent terms, allowing analysts to observe clusters of terms, subjects, topics, or themes in collections or "corpuses" of text (DiMaggio, Nag, & Blei, 2013:578). Sociologists of culture have usefully applied automated text models to distill media frames (DiMaggio et al., 2013), sentiments (Joseph, Wei, Benigni, & Carley, 2016), strategic motives (Mohr, Wagner-Pacifici, Breiger, & Bogdanov, 2013), and political-cultural logics (Nelson, 2018). This suggests that these models will also be useful for detecting clusters of themes deployed or adorned by organizations (Sallaz, 2012).

Findings from our analysis suggest organizations actively signal distinct themes under a broader ideological umbrella to potential recruits through the use of various symbols of terror. While both publications are produced with high-resolution images and professional English prose, there are major differences between them. Major themes in AQAP's publication Inspire paint the image of an organization seeking to radicalize, inform, and "inspire" would-be terrorists by focusing on war-time events, U.S. and coalition attributed attacks on civilians, messages from leaders, and notably, instructions for individuals to create weapons with spotlights on high profile targets of value. In contrast, ISIS's English language publications Dabiq and Rumiyah focus on themes relating to historical/religious justifications, legitimacy of the caliphate, war-time events frequently exaggerating ISIS's strategic position, and-for a more personal touch-letters from families of ISIS fighters including interviews with fighters themselves. These differences are stark and patterned, constructing holistic identities through packages of themes designed to appeal to a pool of people (potential recruits) who rebuke institutional or state-based arbiters of legitimacy, and instead react to symbols of illegitimacy.

sections.paper_id: Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
sections.header_raw: Terrorist propaganda as an enduring theming device
sections.header_norm: terrorist propaganda as an enduring theming device
paragraphs: Developed through a merger of Saudi and Yemeni affiliate organizations in early 2009, Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP) is widely considered the one of the most lethal Al-Qaeda linked organizations (CFR Staff, 2015). Working to exploit instability in Yemen following years of U.S. counterterrorism operations and political upheaval following the Arab Spring, AQAP has developed a wide range of media techniques to attract potential members (CFR Staff, 2015). These techniques range from the routine publication of English and Arabic language magazines, an extensive social media campaign, and direct communication from AQAP leadership directed towards would-be terrorists in Western countries. AQAP began publishing their English language magazine Inspire in 2010 in an apparent effort to promote lone-wolf terrorist activity in English speaking nations (Ingram, 2017). This effort drew the attention of Western policymakers and media outlets due in large part to the high-resolution, professional quality photographs, and writings communicating sensitive content. In addition to the attention from the West, Inspire was seen as a prototype for other terrorist organizations to develop their own English language magazine targeting Western audiences (CFR Staff, 2015).

Similarly, ISIS emerged in earnest following the withdrawal of U.S. troops from Iraq in 2010, and the subsequent destabilization in Syria due to civil war (CFR Staff, 2015). The organization experienced a great deal of success on the battlefield in 2014, expanding rapidly into Eastern Iraq by taking Mosul-Iraq's second most populous city. Along with military success came an effort to attract foreign fighters through a variety of social media platforms as well as the use of targeted propaganda. To this end, ISIS launched Dabiq in 2014 in an effort to ostensibly report on the military situation at any given time and to recruit foreign-fighters to join the effort. Similar to Inspire, Dabiq (later rebranded to Rumiyah) features high quality photographs and professional writing. These characteristics have drawn attention from U.S. policy makers and media as an important opportunity to understand the "incredible recruiting successes of the Islamic State (ISIS)" (McCoy, 2014).

Content analyses of the Dabiq and Inspire have yielded important insights into how specific meanings are imparted to target audiences. Ingram (2017) demonstrates how these magazines offer a "lens" through which readers interpret and perceive the world. Broadly writ, the content targets Muslims and fundamentalist sympathizers living in Western nations by offering a repudiation of Western values while cultivating a sense of nostalgia for traditional ways of life that are perceived to be under attack or in crisis. Recruits are presumably attracted to narratives of tradition, in-group identity, and perceptions of certain triumphant success. These narratives are based largely on a "political mythology" that dramatizes notions of constant, inevitable, and historically informed socio-political clashes between Western "crusaders" and true believers (Kirke, 2015). For example, Christien (2016) argues that ISIS displays images and stories about young people engaged in violent roles in order to radicalize a new generation of youth, while simultaneously critiquing modernist paradigms with regard to children who are often represented as innocent, non-agentic victims of conflict. Similarly, other research on terrorist propaganda has demonstrated that AQAP and ISIS pursue divergent military tactics (Novenario, 2016). Novenario asserts that AQAP uses logics of "attrition" or strategies geared towards signaling to the enemy that terrorists are willing and capable of inflicting damage if the enemy does not change its behavior. In contrast, ISIS focuses on logics of intimidation, or strategies of social control through narratives emphasizing battlefield victories and military strength and narratives emphasizing high levels of allegiance or sacrifice for the cause in order to outbid rival organizations.

While the focus on terrorist identity has provided a fruitful comparison of terrorist propaganda at the participant level, we are interested in the useful construction of stable and enduring themes at the organizational level. Organizational sociologists have developed additional theoretical perspectives towards understanding how organizations strategically use themes to signal legitimacy (Sallaz, 2012), or in certain circumstances, strategic illegitimacy to achieve culturally specific legitimation (Schoon, 2014). While research has argued that Inspire and Dabiq/Rumiyah work in similar ways to attract potential recruits (see Ingram, 2017), it ignores that these organizations are in direct competition for a discrete pool of potential new members. As a result, we assume that terrorist groups theme themselves in different ways in an effort to distinguish themselves from rival organizations. Thus, these periodicals serve as primary tools utilized by AQAP and ISIS to communicate that they are one specific type of terrorist organization as opposed to another.

sections.header_raw: Organizational adornment as a legitimacy strategy
sections.header_norm: organizational adornment as a legitimacy strategy
paragraphs: The combination of designs, logos, symbols, and words to construct a holistic organizational brand is one of the foundational components of organizational communication (Mumby, 2008). In isolation, each of these branding components represents an opportunity to communicate organizational identity by coordinating various design elements or adornments such as visual (e.g. artwork), textual (e.g. narratives), and audible (e.g. songs or jingles) technologies to convey a concentrated "theme" (Simonson & Schmitt, 2009). Using themes to signal distinction from competitors in the market field is a fundamental component of what business scholars refer to as the "experience economy" (Pine & Gilmore et al., 1999). Beyond competing on the basis of price, research on how to tap into consumer preferences, and more importantly align with them, has come to characterize the rational-economic approach to delivering products infused with symbolic values in efforts to garner market advantage (Schechter, 1993). In other words, organizations seek to strategically market their aesthetics, or their "look and feel," in order to appeal to consumer wants while simultaneously eliciting enduring loyalty (Simonson & Schmitt, 2009). In a commercial sense, creating value (profit) for an organization is a combination of shrewd pricing decisions coupled with clever themes designed to convey the experience a consumer receives as a byproduct of their purchase (Sundbo & Darmer, 2008). For example, rational-economic proponents would argue that it is not simply pragmatic decisions about fuel economy that would dictate a person's choice of vehicle, but also the ability of a manufacturer to successfully market their brand as fashionable in order to engineer consumers to recognize an opportunity to actualize themselves through their purchases.

This approach has come under fire from sociologists who conceive of market actors as constrained in their behaviors and choices. Instead of seeing themes as a hodgepodge of calculated attempts at achieving product distinction, the neoinstitutional approach argues that how an organization presents itself is an outcome of "highly structured organizational fields" that limit the ability of actors to act strategically (DiMaggio & Powell, 1983:147). Organizations succumb to isomorphic pressures stemming from global norms of behavior that displace efforts to act efficiently in favor of efforts to act legitimate (Meyer & Rowan, 1977;DiMaggio & Powell, 1983). These normative conditions are reproduced and diffused leading to increasingly similar organizations. With regards to conveying symbols and meanings to consumers, neoinstitutionalists have been strong advocates for understanding the formation of consumer preferences, going so far as to argue that organizational forms are an outcome of a central "logic" linked at the social/institutional level (Friedland & Alford, 1991). As a result, cultivating the visible aspects of a particular organizational form-such as logos, brand names, or in this case, terrorist themes-is more likely the result of interdependent expectations of various stakeholders at the field level (e.g. peer organizations, elites, consumers etc.) rather than savvy individual entrepreneurship (DiMaggio, 1991;Meyer & Jepperson, 2000).

Scholars have recently transitioned away from viewing organizational adornment as either rational or extra-rational, and instead advocated for a synthesis of these dominant approaches. Neither purely a response to market supply/demand mechanisms or isomorphic tendencies stripping actors of choice, it is more useful to frame market activities as a product of overlapping fields of production (Fligstein & Calder, 2001;Fligstein, 1996). Fligstein (1996) advocates for a markets-as-politics approach understanding the market as a both a competitive and coordinated space. Formally defined, markets-as-politics is a metaphor for the interrelated processes of state-building and struggles to control markets by powerful actors in relief of dominant institutions (Fligstein, 1996:657). In other words, market actors are constrained by the formal legal boundaries that define the limits of available action, while operating in a system in which competing actors try to signal legitimacy to navigate those constraints. Thus, the competing attempts by actors to frame actions as legitimate, and consequently others as illegitimate, becomes a "field" or arena of market struggle. What types of practices become legitimated are a consequence of: 1. consensus on what is considered appropriate, and 2. effective governance mechanisms that sustain legitimation. Though primarily concerned with conceptualizing profit-driven markets of production, markets-as-politics can similarly be useful towards understanding symbolic or ideologically-driven markets of production. To this end, the markets-as-politics approach might argue that it is not simply the aggregate relationships between terrorist organizations and recruits (e.g. rational economic approach), or a homogenous global terrorist culture providing the template for legitimate action (e.g. neoinstitutional approach), but rather a dynamic interplay between various political, cultural, and market fields that diffuse multiple logics of "doing" terrorism.

Recent research adopting the markets-as-politics approach argues that the theming process can be thought of as a strategy of "organizational adornment," where organizations adopt combinations of themes to signal political and cultural legitimacy while remaining competitive in a market environment (Sallaz, 2012). As Sallaz demonstrates, organizational legitimacy in the casino industry stems from the combination of globally diffused logics of competition while also adhering to local contexts informing organizational practices. The success or failure of particular casinos are related to packaging themes that simultaneously signal legitimacy to multiple stakeholders across and within fields of competition. As a result, organizational survival is often related to the successful balance of signaling fit within the local political/cultural environment which the casino is directly embedded in, while remaining accountable to broader forces of institutional governance that shape the rules of the casino industry. Organizational adornment as a theoretical tool has been evidenced through ethnographic observation and interview data to make an empirical caseyet interpretive analyses of the primary materials of organizations, such as terrorist propaganda magazines, are ripe for analysis as well.

Instead of assuming terrorist organizations develop private incentives to meet the established private demands of recruits in high risk fields (see Olson, 1965), we argue that terrorist groups strategically supply themes to a pool of potential recruits that are embedded in overlapping political and cultural fields. The markets-as-politics paradigm suggests that organizations should compete in an environment legitimated by government actors. However, terrorist organizations seek legitimacy from sources beyond global or local political authority by presenting coherent visions to sympathizers of what it means to do terrorism (Olzak, 2016). Themes are indicative of organizational adornment strategies targeting recruits and motivating them to do terrorism in the way that they do terrorism. Given that the state is unable to act as the arbiter of legitimate competition among terrorist organizations, as they almost universally operate outside of the institutional political process, potential recruits respond to particular adornment strategies that signal distinct ideological identities by choosing winners and losers among differing visions for doing terrorism (Olzak, 2016). In this way, terrorist organizations are legitimated by recruits, while maintaining, and even flaunting their illegitimacy in the eyes of the state. This plays on a careful tension between the expectations among several stakeholders-recruits, donors, the state-in order to achieve strategic goals. While terrorist organizations in some ways might work to appease local, and at times, global authorities, they are explicitly concerned with the pragmatic challenges of recruiting a sizeable enough force to meaningfully operate and survive. As a result, we argue that global terrorist organizations engage in cross-national recruitment efforts and use adornment strategies to draw distinctions between themselves and rival organizations.

Within the terrorist field, Islamic fundamentalism remains a prominent ideological category. Previous scholarship to this end has argued that the field of radical Islamic terrorism should not be seen as a homogenous ideological whole, but rather a flexible arrangement of ideas and discursive frames that enable effective mobilization (Snow & Byrd, 2007). A key component of the extant social movements literature on organizational framing is understanding how movements strategically deploy frames to different audiences in an effort to gain legitimacy (Blee & McDowell, 2012). The contentious politics literature on terrorist organizations has echoed a similar call for understanding how revolutionary organizations mobilize ideas in an effort to appeal to key constituents (Beck, 2008;Grynkewich, 2008;Bakker et al., 2012;Chou, 2016;Schoon, 2015;Olzak, 2016). Schoon (2015)'s descriptive analysis of the Kurdistan Workers' Party in Turkey (PKK) evidenced how illegitimate revolutionary organizations can strategically evoke subidentities towards multiple conflicting sources of legitimacy in an effort to adhere to a broader issue frame and secure successful outcomes. Similarly, Olzak (2016) demonstrates the benefits of conveying a crisp ideology for promoting the longevity and lethality of terrorist organizations, and also for effectively signaling identity to potential recruits. However, less is known about intra-category identity differences between what social movement scholars refer to as "spin-off" organizations who may occupy a clear ideological niche but demonstrate ideological commitment in strikingly different ways (ibid. 2016). There is good reason to believe that there is a great deal of variation in terms of the way that radical Islamic terrorist organizations theme themselves, even among those within the same broad ideological tent.

Proposition: While broadly operating under the category of radical Islamic terrorist organization, AQAP and ISIS will use propaganda to signal intra-category differences through themes that highlight proprietary, organizationally specific aspects of terrorist activity and identity.

AQAP's focus on specifically western, English-speaking audiences suggests that they may actively adorn their organization with themes related to political-cultural events concerning a potential recruits' place of origin. An example of this would be constructing themes that highlight successful, high profile lone-wolf terrorist activities carried out by AQAP members abroad. In contrast, ISIS's self-identified sovereignty effort and focus on recruiting participants from a variety of English and nonnative English-speaking nations to join military efforts as foreign fighters suggests that they may actively adorn their organization with themes related to political-cultural events to appeal to potential recruits who are sympathetic ISIS's ideological and military struggles abroad. An example of this would be constructing themes that highlight scripture-based justifications for military actions in their war effort. These distinctions, while broadly remaining under the umbrella category of radical Islamic terrorism, should demonstrate specific organizational goals informing potential recruits about how each respective Islamic terrorist organization cultivates an identity.

sections.paper_id: Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
paragraphs: Inspire is the theme building media for AQAP, an English language, online, publicly available, digital magazine featuring highresolution images and literature. First published in 2010 as a part of a larger terror movement popularly referred to as "open source jihad," Inspire commonly advocates for individualized methods of terrorism including lone-wolf attacks (Wiskind, 2016). The latest issue was released in September 2015 following the infamous Charlie Hebdo shooting that occurred in France. To date, a total of sixteen issues have been published. In contrast, the multilingual magazine Dabiq (and more recently Rumiyah) serves as the primary propaganda resource for ISIS. First published in 2014, Dabiq is primarily proliferated over the deep-web as a strategic messaging device for the terrorist cell. As opposed to advocating for unitary action, Dabiq actively focuses on religiously motivated sovereignty and measures for promoting collective legitimacy (Gambhir, 2014). With Turkish forces taking control of the Syrian city of Dabiq, and the death of prominent ISIS spokesperson Abu Mohammad al-Adnani in August of 2016, Dabiq has since been rebranded as an identical periodical titled Rumiyah (Arabic for Rome). A total of fifteen issues of Dabiq were published up until July 2016, with an additional five issues of Rumiyah released until the most recent publication in January 2017. Documents were retrieved online through the public clearing house jihodology.net. Subsequently, we utilized optical character recognition software (OCR) to convert the. pdf documents into text files. To preprocess the text for analysis, we utilized the NLTK 3.0 package to remove common noise words that on their own are not helpful for interpreting themes (e.g. "the", "is", "but", etc.) and also generated a custom list of noise words that removed unintelligible characters leftover from the OCR process or Arabic words translated to English (e.g. "nasarahullah", "nahl", etc.).

To preprocess the text for analysis, we utilized the NLTK 3.0 package to remove common noise words that on their own are not helpful for interpreting themes (e.g. "the", "is", "but", etc.) and also generated a custom list of noise words that removed unintelligible characters leftover from the OCR process or Arabic words translated to English (e.g. "nasarahullah", "nahl", etc.).
sections.preview_head: Inspire is the theme building media for AQAP, an English language, online, publicly available, digital magazine featuring highresolution images and literature. First published in 2010 as a part of a larger terror movement popularly referred to as "open source jihad," Inspire commonly advocates for individualized methods of terrorism including lone-wolf attacks (Wiskind, 2016). The latest issue was released in September 2015 following the infamous Charlie Hebdo shooting that occurred in France.

sections.paper_id: Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
paragraphs: The goals of this paper are twofold: 1. to evaluate how each respective organization adopts independent themes and offer a formal comparison between adornment strategies, and 2. build on prior attempts at understanding the nature of jihadist propaganda by offering a more rigorous, less biased investigation into the corpus of literature distributed by two of the top operating terrorist organizations. To this end, this paper utilizes increasingly popular methods of computer-assisted text analysis techniques developed by computer scientists to overcome implicit bias in content interpretation (Nelson, 2018).

To evaluate within organization adornment strategies, we utilize Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) which is a probabilistic topic modeling strategy for estimating topics within text (Blei et al., 2003). This method is only one type of machine-learned topic modeling procedure but is preferred for modeling texts with diverse themes (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). The basic premise of LDA assumes that the composition of a corpus of texts (in this case magazine issues) is structured from random mixture topics (or "themes") represented by words. The goal of this procedure is to mathematically discover common themes across magazine issues. Themes in this case are formally defined under the LDA method as "a distribution [of words] over a fixed category" (Blei, 2012:78). For example, the theme "Bombing" in an issue may contain a collection of words such as "explosion", "blast", and "detonation". The LDA algorithm assumes that themes are present before the collection of words in the document and predicts clusters of words based on a document-term matrix based on raw word counts in the magazine issues. Each magazine issue is assumed to contain the same set of themes in different proportions. The observed model output represents the hidden or latent structure of the magazines drawn from the observed collection of words.

Following Gaby and Caren (2016) we experimented with different cut points for extracting themes (e.g. ten, twenty-five, thirty etc.). The feature selection for these models was rendered based on term-frequency (Bansal, 2016). After running these test models, the most coherent and interpretable cutoff point across both document corpuses was 12 themes with 1600 features. 1 Themes reported below were selected based on their prominence across documents. Once LDA was applied to each magazine corpus, we extracted the top fifty bigram features from the sample utilized in the generation of the themes. Words that co-occur most often reflect the most distinctive features or theming devices utilized by each organization. In this way, we are able to understand how each terrorist organization leans on certain expressions as more or less representative of each organizational adornment strategy.

Though term-frequencies on their own are useful for distilling categories, calculating a difference-of-proportions offers a less biased way to compare thematic frames between organizations (Table 1).

paragraphs: Table 2 presents the unigram themes representing the corpus of Inspire and Dabiq/Rumiyah. Unigrams (or single term models) provide term-groups that are independent of semantic structure and are a useful starting point for comparison (Blei et al., 2003). Representative terms are grouped in a manner that hints at holistic topics while preserving the potential for topic similarities among organizations. Themes are ordered by their prevalence across documents. This means that the first topic in the table is the most prevalent theme across each respective corpus, where the last topic is the least prevalent theme across each respective corpus. Following, DiMaggio et al. ( 2013) and Gaby and Caren (2016) we inductively chose theme labels in the first row based on the shared meaning of commonly occurring words in each topic. We explored the meanings undergirding each term through reading through independent research into particular terms and reading the texts themselves. Words in the table are ordered by vectorized count (e.g. term frequency). As a result, some words may appear across topics such as the word "lone" in the themes and "Open Source Jihad" and "Assassination" for Inspire. This indicates similarities between themes used to develop unique organizational adornment strategies (e.g. "Open Source Jihad" as a tactical philosophy and "Assassination" as a tactical application). At the same time, these unique words remain contextually important as more or less representative of each theme. 2As expected, AQAP and ISIS articulate competing visions of legitimate terrorist activity. In the corpus for Inspire, the top theme "Open Source Jihad" alludes to a particular type of terrorist philosophy relying on easy, reproducible attacks carried out by individuals engrained in society rather than as soldiers of AQAP living in the Arabian Peninsula. This is complimented "Improvised Materials", a less prevalent theme containing terms describing accessible, inexpensive materials utilized in improvised explosive devices (IED's), and "Assassination" describing an approach to carrying out open source jihad. These themes demonstrate how AQAP adorns itself with a "do-it-yourself" approach to participation by emphasizing tools for lone-wolf actors to commit terrorist activities. "Open Source Jihad" contains the terms "lone" (151), "boston" (88), "marathon" (42), and-the acronym for open source jihad-"osj" (34). This elicits both the philosophy and application of the approach by hinting at the high-profile 2013 Boston Marathon bombing carried out by the Tsarnaev brothers using homemade bombs. Though As a part of a general adornment strategy, AQAP engages in efforts to highlight low-cost strategies of participation by promoting an increasingly necessary set of tactics developed due to AQAP's threatened brand due to internal factionalization (Merriam, 2011).

For Dabiq/Rumiyah, the top two themes are directly concerned with categorical geopolitical and ideological boundaries between ISIS and perceived religious and nation-state competitors. "Religious Boundaries" containing the top term "faction" (198) and "Political Boundaries" with the same top term "faction" (98) indicates a textual emphasis on describing boundary work to foreign fighters who would be receptive to organized ideological/political efforts ISIS is known for (CFR Staff, 2015). Despite that both themes are concerned with factions, they focus on distinct contexts for cultivating these boundaries by blurring the lines between nationalist and divine rhetoric. In the theme "Religious Boundaries", the second term "sharia" (198) is tied with the top term and is followed by "jawlani" (141) hinting at the Jawlani Front or Al-Qaeda in Syria, an organization considered a chief ideological rival of ISIS's "caliphate" (94) (MacDonald, 2015). These terms are coupled with allusions to religious competitors with the terms "god" (128), "jesus" (120), and "pagan" (70).

In contrast, "Political Boundaries" is constructed from oppositional state territories terms "syria" (71), "libya" (57) and "iran" (48) coupled with patriotic terms "nationalist" (37) and "political" (37) to highlight ISIS's perception that their organization represents an effort to construct a legitimate independent state. Though less prevalent across the corpus, ISIS supports these top themes by outlining direct military efforts against rival nations and ideologies in the themes "Counterinsurgency" and "Mid-East Operations." The theme "Counterinsurgency" uses the scripture-based term "wilayah" (45) meaning proximate nation to support top terms "vehicle" (101) and "explosive" (65) highlighting military conflict. Similarly, the theme "Mid-East Operations" uses the term "yarmuk" (29) describing the famous historical battle between the Byzantine Empire and the Rashidun Caliphate in 639 CE to draw parity to contemporary efforts in the "arabian" (33) "peninsula" (36). To reduce bias and overcome inferential limitations of unigram models, we drew a sample of bigram topic models that incorporate a semantic component.foot_1 Table 3 presents these word sequences to contextualize some of the unigram themes and introduce greater richness regarding AQAP and ISIS's adornment strategies. At the bigram level, themes in Inspire become a "greatest hits" of lone-wolf terrorism. The top theme "Boston Bombing" contains the terms "lone jihad" (31), "boston marathon" ( 22), and "pressure cooker" (19) to highlight the philosophy, the attack site, and the method for AQAP's preferred brand of terrorism. Though less prevalent, the themes "Charlie Hebdo Massacre" describing the 2015 attack on the French satire magazine, and "Christmas Day Bomb Plot" describing the nuances of the infamous attempted bombing of Northwest Airlines Flight 253, further outline a preference for highlighting high-profile, individualized terror acts in their effort to recruit new participants.

For Dabiq/Rumiyah, the bigram models further evidence efforts to develop an organizational adornment strategy related to sovereignty and religious boundaries. Specifically, the top theme "Syrian Conflict" containing the terms "jawlani front" (61), "syrian army" (20), "free syrian" (20), and "syrian people" (15) highlights the ongoing civil war between belligerents in Syria-noting the Jawlani Front and the Free Syrian Army who were in conflict with ISIS in the region. The second most prevalent theme "Territory", is complemented by a package of less prevalent themes related to religious motivated militancy in "Jihad Operations", "Holy War" and "Jihadism". Within the topic of "Jihad Operations", the terms "detonated explosive" (15), "killing injured" (11), "waging jihad" (10), and "wage war" (8) are coupled with "ahlus sunnah" (13) referring to the Sunni Islamic faith, and "lord creation" ( 9). This frames a distinct effort to define ISIS's geopolitical struggles as a dual conquest to institute a singular view of Islam and create a unified Islamic state under the guise fundamentalist interpretations of the Muslim faith.

One pronounced overlap the bigram level are the themes "Jihad Operations" and "Jihadism." Though AQAP and ISIS are unified in an effort to further a fundamentalist agenda, the term distinction suggests that their jihadist philosophy and tactical applications are dissimilar. For AQAP, the theme "Jihad Operations" utilizes the terms "open front" (16), "guerilla warfare" (8), "fard ayn" (7) -an Arabic term for individual, and "islamic nation" (7) suggesting that their applications of jihad are rooted in the aggregate efforts of individual participants to demonstrate a unified global fundamentalist initiative. This is underscored their theme "Jihadism" that outlines support and solidarity with other global jihadists with prayer terms such as "may mercy" (20), "mercy upon" (11) and "peace upon" (5). This relates to AQAP's effort to adorn themselves with a jihadist philosophy rooted spanning national boundaries.

In contrast, ISIS utilizes tactical terms in "Jihad Operations" such as "detonated explosive" (15), "explosive vehicle" (9), "guided missile" (8) and "explosive vest" (7) focusing on day-to-day military struggles. These standouts are coupled with scripture-based terms in the theme "Jihadism" such as "dhul hijjah" (20)-the final month of the islamic calendar when the traditional pilgrimage to Mecca takes place, and "pillar islam" (3) to appeal to a refined set of fundamentalists that share a unified, traditionalist conception of both the meaning and implementation of jihad.

To specifically investigate the contours of each respective adornment strategy, we extracted the top fifty bigram features in each corpus (Table 4). On the surface, the features for both AQAP and ISIS are concerned with the implementation of fundamentalist conflict. Inspire is saturated with terms such as "lone jihad" (78), "jihad arabian" (56), "jihadi operation" (31), and "individual jihad" (28) suggesting that themes are constructed to recruit lone-wolf participants with a religious directive. Similarly, Dabiq/Rumiyah incorporates the terms "jihad claimant" (94), "soldier islamic" (87), "jihad cause" (53), "crusader coalition" (49), and "fight islamic" (38) to support themes by justifying actions with faith. Looking closer at these features reveals some distinct differences. AQAP adopts a strategy of adornment by constructing themes with globalist terms like "around world" (45), "foreign policy" (34), "iraq afghanistan" (32), "open front," (32), and two allusions to the United States in "american people" (54), and "new york" (54). In contrast, ISIS adopts a strategy of adornment by constructing themes with regionalist terms like "jawlani front" (143), "free syrian" (48), "sahwah coalition" (41), and "syrian people" (41), alluding to the struggle to establish a nation state with a strong foothold in Syria and the immediate surrounding areas.

Though LDA models and feature extraction offer intuitive examples of organizational adornment, one could argue that interpreting themes without making a formal comparison ignores the power of semantic choice in conveying meaning. To this end, we explicitly model semantic choice through the difference-of-proportions test. Fig. 1 offers this formal comparison to distill the most distinct terms used in these adornment strategies. A higher positive score on the x-axis indicates a greater salience of terms present in Inspire, where a higher negative score indicates a greater salience of terms present in Dabiq/Rumiyah.

In Dabiq/Rumiyah. The difference plot confirms the interpretations of the unigram and bigram LDA models. There is a distinct focus on sovereignty for ISIS with the terms "islamic" and "state" coupled closely beyond the -1 mark. For AQAP, there is a distinct focus on anti-American sentiment not present within ISIS's propaganda with the terms "america", "american", and "enemy" all demonstrating considerable contrast in both proportion and frequency in the corpus. Further, the difference plot adds additional support to our contention that Inspire cultivates a theme of open source, lone-wolf terrorism that is not a point of appeal for ISIS with the use of terms such as "lone", "method", and "martyrdom" clustering together in both proportion and frequency between 0-.5 along the x-axis. Finally, ISIS presents clear themes of Islamic-based historical justification in their efforts to attract participants. The terms "islam", "prophet", and "religion" all cluster closely with a marginal difference in proportion, but this is bolstered by "crusader" and "soldier" showing a greater contrast in the plot given its larger frequency score. This serves as stronger evidence that despite both organizations presenting themes of fundamentalism, ISIS's adornment strategy relies on historical-religious themes, while AQAP's adornment strategy relies on a practical, "how-to" themes attempting to inspire lone-wolf actors to perform terrorist activities in their home country.

At the bigram level, themes in Inspire become a "greatest hits" of lone-wolf terrorism. The top theme "Boston Bombing" contains the terms "lone jihad" (31), "boston marathon" ( 22), and "pressure cooker" (19) to highlight the philosophy, the attack site, and the method for AQAP's preferred brand of terrorism. Though less prevalent, the themes "Charlie Hebdo Massacre" describing the 2015 attack on the French satire magazine, and "Christmas Day Bomb Plot" describing the nuances of the infamous attempted bombing of Northwest Airlines Flight 253, further outline a preference for highlighting high-profile, individualized terror acts in their effort to recruit new participants.

For Dabiq/Rumiyah, the bigram models further evidence efforts to develop an organizational adornment strategy related to sovereignty and religious boundaries. Specifically, the top theme "Syrian Conflict" containing the terms "jawlani front" (61), "syrian army" (20), "free syrian" (20), and "syrian people" (15) highlights the ongoing civil war between belligerents in Syria-noting the Jawlani Front and the Free Syrian Army who were in conflict with ISIS in the region. The second most prevalent theme "Territory", is complemented by a package of less prevalent themes related to religious motivated militancy in "Jihad Operations", "Holy War" and "Jihadism". Within the topic of "Jihad Operations", the terms "detonated explosive" (15), "killing injured" (11), "waging jihad" (10), and "wage war" (8) are coupled with "ahlus sunnah" (13) referring to the Sunni Islamic faith, and "lord creation" ( 9). This frames a distinct effort to define ISIS's geopolitical struggles as a dual conquest to institute a singular view of Islam and create a unified Islamic state under the guise fundamentalist interpretations of the Muslim faith.

One pronounced overlap the bigram level are the themes "Jihad Operations" and "Jihadism." Though AQAP and ISIS are unified in an effort to further a fundamentalist agenda, the term distinction suggests that their jihadist philosophy and tactical applications are dissimilar. For AQAP, the theme "Jihad Operations" utilizes the terms "open front" (16), "guerilla warfare" (8), "fard ayn" (7) -an Arabic term for individual, and "islamic nation" (7) suggesting that their applications of jihad are rooted in the aggregate efforts of individual participants to demonstrate a unified global fundamentalist initiative. This is underscored their theme "Jihadism" that outlines support and solidarity with other global jihadists with prayer terms such as "may mercy" (20), "mercy upon" (11) and "peace upon" (5). This relates to AQAP's effort to adorn themselves with a jihadist philosophy rooted spanning national boundaries.

In contrast, ISIS utilizes tactical terms in "Jihad Operations" such as "detonated explosive" (15), "explosive vehicle" (9), "guided missile" (8) and "explosive vest" (7) focusing on day-to-day military struggles. These standouts are coupled with scripture-based terms in the theme "Jihadism" such as "dhul hijjah" (20)-the final month of the islamic calendar when the traditional pilgrimage to Mecca takes place, and "pillar islam" (3) to appeal to a refined set of fundamentalists that share a unified, traditionalist conception of both the meaning and implementation of jihad.

Fig. 1 offers this formal comparison to distill the most distinct terms used in these adornment strategies. A higher positive score on the x-axis indicates a greater salience of terms present in Inspire, where a higher negative score indicates a greater salience of terms present in Dabiq/Rumiyah.

In Dabiq/Rumiyah. The difference plot confirms the interpretations of the unigram and bigram LDA models. There is a distinct focus on sovereignty for ISIS with the terms "islamic" and "state" coupled closely beyond the -1 mark. Finally, ISIS presents clear themes of Islamic-based historical justification in their efforts to attract participants. This serves as stronger evidence that despite both organizations presenting themes of fundamentalism, ISIS's adornment strategy relies on historical-religious themes, while AQAP's adornment strategy relies on a practical, "how-to" themes attempting to inspire lone-wolf actors to perform terrorist activities in their home country.

sections.paper_id: Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
paragraphs: As an extension of prior work investigating organizational adornment (Sallaz, 2012), this paper demonstrates that dissident organizations rely on institutionalized patterns of theming as a method to signal meaning towards potential recruits. The markets-aspolitics approach helps to explain both the similarities and differences embedded terrorist theming. For example, AQAP and ISIS both seek to convey that they are a legitimate, organized terrorist group by focusing on themes of illegitimacy related to state enemies, framing religiosity, and tactical discourse. However, AQAP cultivates these themes with terms focusing on individualized approaches to terrorism, where ISIS explicitly focuses on group and community level appeals. Previous work has argued that the state is the ultimate grantor of institutional legitimacy (Sallaz, 2012). In contrast, this study demonstrates that the inherent illegitimacy of terrorism leads these organizations to rely on various combinations of political, cultural, and market logics in order to appeal to potential consumers (e.g. terrorist recruits) as the arbiters of legitimacy. Thus, decisions to join one group as opposed to the other, or even join any group at all are related to how effective AQAP and ISIS are at signaling to recruits that their respective organization represents: 1. an avenue of ideological actualization, 2. widely accepted terrorist practice, and 3. a recognized effort by various political factions. As a result, we argue that future studies utilizing the markets-as-politics approach carefully consider that when behaviors or governance structures are contested, organizations will seek out alternative audiences to demonstrate legitimacy.

While terrorist organizations provide an instructive case for the markets-as-politics paradigm, this perspective powerfully connects to a budding literature bridging organizational sociology and the sociology of political violence, insurgency, and terrorism (Breiger, Schoon, Melamed, Asal, & Rethemeyer, 2014;Davis, 2017;Olzak, 2016;Parkinson, 2013;Staniland, 2014). Research under this umbrella typically attempts to analyze violent organizations not as though they are deviant cases, but rather as exhibiting similar lone wolf qaeda arabian took part may protect waging war child israil guided missile dangerous bidah blood wealth relied upon public key execute operation foreign policy fard ayn people knowledge western country syrian people one third city bab people scripture pillar islam reported authority middle east peace upon support israel path jihad brother told day judgement ash shami ahlus sunnah took control took place built upon state established jew christian remote control may accept would like military operation security measure fight islamic pakistani army explosive vest land islam islam without shaykul islam

The order of the themes in the table corresponds to its prevalence across documents in decreasing order related to its respective dirichlet parameter. For the bigram model statistics for each corpus please see the supplemental material online.

patterns of behavior to formal organizations in Western contexts. This analysis helps us to understand how terrorist organizations deploy marketing strategies that are usually thought of as reserved for non-violent organizations-communicating organizational identity through digital propaganda in a targeted and thoughtful way. In addition, this analysis allows us to theorize that terrorist propaganda results from a complex web of opportunities and constraints, namely, the need to communicate organizational identity to recruits while facing both competitive pressures from rival terror organizations and pressures from the global community in the form of military force and diplomatic sanctions.

While the ecological tradition has offered useful contributions to an organizational sociology of political violence, this work has typically leveraged rigidly partitioned categories of organizational identity to classify terrorist organizations. For example, AQAP and ISIS would typically be considered as members of an "Islamic-Religious" category as opposed to several other ideological categories such as "Ethno-Nationalist" or "Anarchist" (Olzak, 2016 p. 566). Our analysis allows us to go deeper by looking at how terrorist organizations use themes to adorn themselves in efforts to strategically communicate heterogeneous identities to a similar audience. Through the markets-as-politics perspective, we are able to demonstrate intra-category differences in adornment strategies that illuminate competing conceptions of control over a broader ideological niche by examining two organizations that analysts typically code as an iteration of Islamic-Religious terrorist groups (Fligstein, 1996). This illustrates important limitations in abstracting organizational identity to the aggregate level, which masks differences in how specific organizations enact identity in light of unique opportunities and constraints.

Our unique research design also challenges previous research arguing that terrorist propaganda is inherently homogenous in its structure and effect. The use of LDA based topic modeling procedures to analyze Inspire and Dabiq/Rumiyah calls into question prior assessments of these documents which have argued that despite fundamental differences in the content of these magazines, AQAP and ISIS are nearly identical in their calculated use of rational choice appeals to potential recruits (Ingram, 2017). Though we do not deny that there is an important rational component to these texts, by moving beyond what terrorist organizations are saying, to how they are saying it reveals stark contrasts in recruiting methodologies. By leveraging the power of LDA topic models to account for both the structure and content of language, we suggest that beyond surface level similarities, terrorist propaganda contains contradictory adornment strategies related to the subject matter and semantic choices. The use of LDA models demonstrates how at times, researchers can gain nuanced insights to help better situate the symbolic meanings embedded in textual data that are missed using standard content analysis methods. In light of this we argue that Inspire and Dabiq/Rumiyah represent more than just a method of broad strategic communication, and more closely resembles a public effort to signal a specific type of terrorism brand to a specific type of recruit. Taking seriously that institutional illegitimacy transcends regional and national boundaries (see Schoon, 2014), we further assert that understanding terrorist recruitment as a global effort necessarily requires sensitivity to the fact that terrorist groups must balance both internal and external stakeholder expectations at the field level. Thus, from a markets-as-politics perspective, we see the semantic patterns generated from these topic models as representative of AQAP and ISIS's attempts at legitimizing practices by reinforcing themes to themselves, signaling themes to state and civilian groups, and most importantly, creating thematic contrasts between each other.

Beyond theoretical and methodological advancements, this work has important policy implications for counter-terrorist operations. These operators should take into account that terrorist organizations exist in a complex market in which different types of recruits respond to a range of different themes provides by organizational propaganda. Moreover, the tactics advocated for in the various propaganda should be closely analyzed as typical of a particular organization. This might help in identifying signature behavior when attributing attacks to a specific organization. In essence, the major distinctions between terrorist organizations detected using topic modeling can provide helpful tools for counter-terrorist initiatives to identify sensitive mechanisms leveraged by terrorist groups and perhaps illuminate patterns in successful recruitment efforts.

This paper has some important limitations. First, in an effort to think reflexively about our research (see Bourdieu & Wacquant, 1992), we recognize that this work is an essentially interpretive exercise. Though we describe terms that emerge in unique patterns between these two organizations, the meanings ascribed to those terms are informed by our experience, standpoint, and assumptions. Finally, other sources of theming exist in the documents we have analyzed, namely photographs, which communicate additional information to consumers beyond text. This last point should be the focus of future research, as more powerful computational tools emerge to analyze photographs should improve and strengthen computational-based research analyzing themes in organizational documents.

Taking seriously that institutional illegitimacy transcends regional and national boundaries (see Schoon, 2014), we further assert that understanding terrorist recruitment as a global effort necessarily requires sensitivity to the fact that terrorist groups must balance both internal and external stakeholder expectations at the field level. Thus, from a markets-as-politics perspective, we see the semantic patterns generated from these topic models as representative of AQAP and ISIS's attempts at legitimizing practices by reinforcing themes to themselves, signaling themes to state and civilian groups, and most importantly, creating thematic contrasts between each other.

Beyond theoretical and methodological advancements, this work has important policy implications for counter-terrorist operations. These operators should take into account that terrorist organizations exist in a complex market in which different types of recruits respond to a range of different themes provides by organizational propaganda. Moreover, the tactics advocated for in the various propaganda should be closely analyzed as typical of a particular organization. This might help in identifying signature behavior when attributing attacks to a specific organization. In essence, the major distinctions between terrorist organizations detected using topic modeling can provide helpful tools for counter-terrorist initiatives to identify sensitive mechanisms leveraged by terrorist groups and perhaps illuminate patterns in successful recruitment efforts.

This paper has some important limitations. First, in an effort to think reflexively about our research (see Bourdieu & Wacquant, 1992), we recognize that this work is an essentially interpretive exercise. Though we describe terms that emerge in unique patterns between these two organizations, the meanings ascribed to those terms are informed by our experience, standpoint, and assumptions. Finally, other sources of theming exist in the documents we have analyzed, namely photographs, which communicate additional information to consumers beyond text. This last point should be the focus of future research, as more powerful computational tools emerge to analyze photographs should improve and strengthen computational-based research analyzing themes in organizational documents.

As an extension of prior work investigating organizational adornment (Sallaz, 2012), this paper demonstrates that dissident organizations rely on institutionalized patterns of theming as a method to signal meaning towards potential recruits. The markets-aspolitics approach helps to explain both the similarities and differences embedded terrorist theming. For example, AQAP and ISIS both seek to convey that they are a legitimate, organized terrorist group by focusing on themes of illegitimacy related to state enemies, framing religiosity, and tactical discourse. However, AQAP cultivates these themes with terms focusing on individualized approaches to terrorism, where ISIS explicitly focuses on group and community level appeals. Previous work has argued that the state is the ultimate grantor of institutional legitimacy (Sallaz, 2012). In contrast, this study demonstrates that the inherent illegitimacy of terrorism leads these organizations to rely on various combinations of political, cultural, and market logics in order to appeal to potential consumers (e.g. terrorist recruits) as the arbiters of legitimacy. Thus, decisions to join one group as opposed to the other, or even join any group at all are related to how effective AQAP and ISIS are at signaling to recruits that their respective organization represents: 1. an avenue of ideological actualization, 2. widely accepted terrorist practice, and 3. a recognized effort by various political factions. As a result, we argue that future studies utilizing the markets-as-politics approach carefully consider that when behaviors or governance structures are contested, organizations will seek out alternative audiences to demonstrate legitimacy.

sections.paper_id: Kinney et al. - 2018 - Theming for terror Organizational adornment in terrorist propaganda
paragraphs: This paper demonstrates the utility of the markets-as-politics as framework to illuminate intra-category differences between organizations within the same ideological niche. Similarly, this paper has extended this research paradigm to analyze propaganda text produced by terrorist organizations, by moving past commercial organizations to show how theming processes can operate across a wide-range of organizations-some illicit. This extension requires future work to consider how the concept of 'legitimacy' can both entail positive and negative behaviors-and how signaling illegitimacy, rather than legitimacy, might prove to be most successful in specific markets. This extension of the concept of organizational adornment to non-commercial organization, particularly terrorist organizations, is a useful elaboration on the original theory.

Additionally, this paper demonstrates the power of topic modeling as a tool for analyzing text produced by terrorist organizations, and in particular online propaganda. As a common outreach method for terrorist organizations attempting to fill their ranks with new members or inspire terrorist attacks, this procedure has been shown to be sensitive to distinctions between organizations in their theming processes where other human or "deep" interpretive readings were unable to perceive such distinctions (see Ingram, 2017). Future research on comparative terrorist organizational identity should look to computational text analytic procedures to capture sensitive distinctions between organizations.
